apps.name: Pairadox
apps.summary: Mix two ingredients into a creative challenge or a funny story with a three-panel symbolic storyboard.
apps.description: A tactile idea game with challenge and story modes. Drag, tap, or use the keyboard to load two ingredients, then create a prompt or a complete local-only comedy with animated symbolic scenes and optional sound.

config.instructions: Drag or tap two ingredients into the forge. Choose Challenge for a prompt or Story for a generated comedy with symbolic scenes, then remix whenever you want another result.
outcomes: Invent a game that uses both ingredients and explain it in one sentence.
Create a thirty-second story in which both ingredients solve the same problem.
Design a fictional product inspired by both ingredients and give it a slogan.
Make a harmless challenge for a friend that includes both ingredients.
Describe a new holiday built around both ingredients.
Turn both ingredients into the rules of a miniature competition.
Imagine both ingredients are clues. Explain the mystery they reveal.
Combine both ingredients into an unusual but believable superpower.
Design a one-button arcade game where both ingredients change the rules.
Create a touch interaction where dragging one ingredient transforms the other.
Invent a sound cue for each ingredient, then combine them into a success jingle.
Turn the pair into a twenty-second physical challenge with three escalating rounds.
Sketch a moving icon that transitions from the first ingredient into the second.
Make a cooperative mini-game playable with one finger from each player.
Design a harmless boss battle where one ingredient is the player and the other is the obstacle.
Add a replay twist that changes the challenge after every imaginary win.

apps.name: Time Sense
apps.summary: Choose a quick or deep session and test how accurately you feel time passing.
apps.description: An arcade-like perception game with three session lengths, a moving canvas spark, hidden timing, optional local sound, scoring, touch, pointer, keyboard support, and clean resource teardown.

config.instructions: Choose Quick, Classic, or Endurance, then start the hidden timer and stop when the target duration feels complete.

apps.name: Fair Choice
apps.summary: Spin an animated fairness wheel that gently reduces repeat winners.
apps.description: A responsive canvas wheel for chores, turns, restaurants, or group choices. It supports touch, pointer, keyboard, optional audio, recent-winner balancing, duplicate protection, and clean cancellation when closed.

config.instructions: Add two to twelve choices, then tap the animated wheel or use the keyboard. Recent winners become temporarily less likely to repeat.

apps.name: Tiny Step
apps.summary: Build a moving three-step trail matched to your goal and current energy.
apps.description: A local-only momentum tool with an animated SVG trail, draggable step selector, touch and keyboard controls, optional audio, flexible completion tracking, and energy-aware actions.

config.instructions: Name the goal, choose your real energy level, then build a three-step trail. Drag the marker, tap a step, or use the keyboard to inspect and complete the route.

apps.name: Constraint Spark
apps.summary: Draw freely or race through three timed creative constraints with one strategic swap.
apps.description: A tactile 16-card constraint deck with Explore and Three-card Sprint modes, 30/60/90-second pacing, scoring, one strategic swap, animated SVG motion, touch, pointer, keyboard controls, optional local audio, and no-repeat reshuffling.

config.instructions: Explore the animated deck freely, or choose a 30, 60, or 90-second three-card sprint. Complete each round for points and use one strategic swap when a constraint does not fit.
items: Make the ending explain the beginning.
Remove the feature you would normally show first.
Use repetition as the organizing principle.
Design for someone using only one hand.
Let one deliberate imperfection remain visible.
Make the smallest useful version possible.
Replace one instruction with a discoverable interaction.
Make one element respond differently to a tap, a drag, and a keyboard input.
Build a loop that becomes harder every thirty seconds.
Use motion to reveal state instead of adding explanatory text.
Let the player discover one rule by causing a harmless failure.
Design the interaction around a single moving object.
Add an optional sound cue that communicates success without words.
Make the experience fully playable with one finger and the arrow keys.
Turn the final state into the starting condition for the next round.

apps.name: Signal Garden
apps.summary: Read harder moving signal patterns across four stations in practice, rush, or survival mode.
apps.description: A local pattern-prediction game with eight pattern families, adaptive difficulty, focus-powered hints, scoring, streaks, lives, timed play, canvas motion, touch, keyboard controls, optional audio, and clean teardown.

config.instructions: Watch signals move among four stations, infer the rule, and predict the next stop. Practice, rush, or survive ten adaptive rounds with focus hints.
prompts: Orbit signals move consistently around the garden.
Echo signals repeat each station before moving on.
Weave signals alternate between two independent paths.
Mirror signals fold back through a short motif.
Pulse signals return to one anchor between changes.
Skip signals leap over one station before settling.
Bloom signals expand outward, then collapse back inward.
Drift signals slow near one station before changing direction.

apps.name: Scene Circuit
apps.summary: Build a six-part scene, then add complications that drive it through reversals, costs, and an earned ending.
apps.description: A staged story lab with six ingredient lenses and a progression arc. Select a core cast, place, pressure, resource, twist, and tone, then add or swap complications to test alternate routes through the same scene.

config.instructions: Choose one chip from each lens to build the core scene. Then add complications one at a time to advance the story beat, or swap a chip to test a different route.
items: Pressure: nobody may name the real problem
Resource: a favor that must be repaid
prompts: Final pass: state the opening image, opposing goals, ticking pressure, decisive reversal, cost of success, final choice, and closing image.
Opening image: show the lead already doing something that reveals a flaw before anyone explains the problem.
Opposition: give two selected ingredients goals that cannot both succeed. Make the conflict visible through action rather than explanation.
Clock: turn the selected pressure into a countdown with a concrete consequence. Show what becomes impossible when time expires.
Rule and temptation: make one ingredient a rule, another the temptation to break it, and a third the price of doing so.
Living setting: let the place change what can be seen, heard, reached, or admitted. The setting must force a choice.
Core scene: combine the six selected lenses into a playable beginning, escalation, decision, and temporary result.
Escalation: add a complication that makes the current plan more attractive and more dangerous at the same time.
False win: let the characters solve the visible problem, then reveal the selected detail that proves the deeper problem remains.
Cost: assign a specific cost to success. Decide who pays it now and who discovers it later.
Reversal: make the least dramatic selected ingredient overturn the current interpretation without invalidating earlier clues.
Role pressure: force the lead and rival to exchange responsibilities. Show what each misunderstands about the other's burden.
Callback: repeat an earlier object, line, or movement with a changed meaning that exposes the emotional shift.
Close the easy exit: remove the obvious resolution and build a smaller, stranger option that still follows from the selected ingredients.
Truth beat: allow one honest sentence. It should solve the emotional question while making the practical problem harder.
Final choice: present two viable endings with different costs. Choose the one that best expresses the lead's changed priority.
Closing image: echo the opening image with one precise difference that proves the scene changed something.
Remix audit: swap one chip and identify every beat that must change. Keep the version where the consequences remain most coherent.
options: Twist: the warning was an invitation
Twist: the rival quietly needs help
Twist: the missing object is visible
Tone: tense with one calm person
Tone: formal behavior in a ridiculous situation

apps.name: Ripple Map
apps.summary: Build a consequence map that exposes delayed costs, feedback loops, hidden dependencies, and exit signals.
apps.description: A local systems-thinking lab for decisions with second-order effects. Add one lens at a time across choice, stakeholder, horizon, pressure, dependency, and signal; each selection advances the analysis toward a reversible test.

config.instructions: Select one chip from each lens in order: choice, stakeholder, horizon, pressure, dependency, and signal. Swap chips to compare strategies.
prompts: Synthesize the map: decision, beneficiaries, costs, delayed consequence, feedback loop, warning signal, and reversible test.
Immediate effect: what changes first, and what stays deceptively unchanged?
Distribution: who gets the benefit, and who quietly inherits extra work, risk, or waiting?
Time shift: how does this horizon change which benefit fades and which cost compounds?
Pressure response: what workaround will people adopt, and what new risk does it create?
Dependency path: where can this dependency delay, distort, or concentrate the effects?
Signal and probe: turn the signal into a stop condition and a reversible test.
Work migration: identify work that appears to disappear but moves elsewhere.
Metric trap: name a number that could improve while experience worsens.
Feedback loop: describe a reinforcing cycle that makes the choice more attractive or costly.
Delayed invoice: what cost arrives only after reversal is difficult?
Asymmetry: who recovers quickly from failure, and who cannot?
Lock-in: what habit, dependency, or expectation becomes harder to unwind?
Recovery design: what must remain intact so the team can retreat?
Missing voice: whose experience is absent from the selected lenses?
No-action comparison: which risks are created by action versus revealed by action?
Smaller option: redesign the choice as a bounded experiment.
Monitoring contract: define signal, outcome, review date, and rollback condition.

apps.name: Threadline
apps.summary: Route a live signal through costly terrain and ordered relays before its charge runs out.
apps.description: A procedural spatial strategy game with three modes, ordered checkpoints, terrain costs, optional caches, limited surges and hints, adaptive difficulty, scoring, canvas motion, drag, touch, pointer, keyboard controls, local audio, and clean teardown.

config.instructions: Trace a route from start to exit, linking numbered relays in order. Manage charge across costly terrain, spend scarce surges and focus, and decide whether optional caches justify a detour.
prompts: Relays must be reached in number order before the exit unlocks.
Brush costs two charge and storm cells cost three.
A surge makes the next valid move cost zero charge.
Focus reveals up to three useful cells toward the next objective.

apps.name: Faultline Drill
apps.summary: Run a tabletop incident drill that exposes weak signals, tradeoffs, handoffs, and recovery paths.
apps.description: A local operational-resilience simulator. Build an incident from six lenses, contain the first failure, stress the response with alternate conditions, and finish with a concrete recovery and learning plan.

config.instructions: Choose one chip from each lens to form the initial incident. Then add or swap alternate conditions to test containment, escalation, recovery, and learning.
prompts: Final debrief: incident, earliest signal, blast radius, containment, tradeoff, recovery path, owner, and prevention.
Normal state: define healthy operation and one assumed dependency.
Detection: separate first signal from first interpretation.
Blast radius: identify who is affected now, next, and what boundary contains spread.
First decision: choose one action that buys information and one that reduces harm.
Scarce resource: assign the constraint to a named decision.
Containment plan: combine six lenses into a fifteen-minute response.
False recovery: symptom clears but cause remains. Which signal reveals danger?
Handoff risk: what context is most likely to be lost?
Second-order failure: show how containment creates a new queue or behavior.
Stakeholder conflict: two stakeholders define success differently.
Metric trap: name a reassuring number that could hide harm.
Escalation threshold: define evidence for shutdown, rollback, or leadership escalation.
No-regrets move: choose an action useful across several causes.
Plan failure: remove one resource and design a smaller recovery route.
Recovery: restore service, validate data, clear workarounds, confirm delayed failures.
Communication contract: write an internal and user-facing update.
Learning loop: convert a signal into an alert, a handoff into a checklist, and an assumption into a fallback.
options: Resource: trusted expert for ten minutes

apps.name: Truce Table
apps.summary: Stage a negotiation with leverage, concessions, hidden risks, and an agreement that survives pressure.
apps.description: A local negotiation simulator for messy group decisions. Build the table from roles, stakes, constraints, offers, risks, and trust signals, then pressure-test the agreement through concessions, walkaways, enforcement, and repair.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to form the negotiation table. Add or swap conditions to test concessions, walkaways, enforcement, repair, and the final agreement.
prompts: Final agreement: real need, conceded item, boundary, enforcement signal, repair path, and next commitment.
Opening positions: list stated wants and quieter needs.
Power map: who can delay, veto, rescue, or undermine the deal?
Constraint pressure: what option becomes impossible and what new option appears?
First concession: build trust without giving away the protected boundary.
Walkaway line: define when continuing causes more damage than pausing.
Package deal: combine six lenses into scope, timing, owner, fallback, and review signal.
Misread motive: correct a false story without embarrassing anyone.
Hidden cost: reveal a cost outside the room.
Trust test: design a reversible action from each party.
Enforcement: separate useful correction from face-saving punishment.
Face-saving exit: let someone change position honestly.
Fallback drill: remove one offer and preserve the core outcome.
Asymmetric risk: who loses fastest if the deal fails?
Signal review: who checks whether the agreement is working and when?
Repair move: restore cooperation without reopening everything.
No-deal comparison: which harm is avoided and which delayed?
Final audit: remove promises without owner, signal, or fallback.

apps.name: Throughput Foundry
apps.summary: Balance demand, capacity, quality, morale, and rework in a bottleneck simulation.
apps.description: A local operations-simulation lab. Select demand, bottleneck, policy, capacity, quality, and morale conditions, then stress-test queues, rework, tradeoffs, recovery, and learning loops.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to model the line. Add or swap conditions to test queues, rework, priority rules, morale, recovery, and throughput.
prompts: Final run: bottleneck, policy, queue behavior, quality risk, morale cost, recovery rule, and proof flow improved.
Baseline: describe one normal unit entering the line.
Arrival pattern: what arrives too early, too late, or in the wrong size?
Constraint map: name the true bottleneck and one tempting non-bottleneck fix.
Policy test: who benefits, who waits, and what behavior does the rule encourage?
Capacity tradeoff: what improves immediately and what cost appears?
First run: combine six lenses into a one-day operating rule.
Queue spiral: what shortcut will people invent when waiting doubles?
Rework loop: reveal a defect that returns work to an earlier station.
Priority conflict: two jobs claim urgency. Decide explicitly.
Morale load: what signal shows burnout before failure?
Batch risk: when does batching hide problems?
Failure drill: remove capacity and degrade gracefully.
Flow experiment: set a work-in-progress limit and review point.
Recovery sequence: what clears first after a jam?
Gaming check: add a balancing measure.
Learning loop: convert signals, policies, and exceptions into earlier decisions.
Final audit: keep the smallest operating model that improves throughput.

apps.name: Evidence Trail
apps.summary: Build and stress-test a case by weighing clues, alternatives, contradictions, and decisive tests.
apps.description: A local investigation lab for sharper reasoning. Select claims, sources, clues, timelines, contradictions, and tests, then pressure-check confidence, missing evidence, rival explanations, and revision paths.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each lens to form the case. Add or swap evidence to test alternatives, confidence, missing facts, and what would change your mind.
prompts: Final case: claim, strongest evidence, weakest assumption, competing explanation, decisive test, and what would change your mind.
Case intake: translate the claim into a testable question.
Source quality: who produced the clue and what incentive may distort it?
Timeline: what must have happened before the visible clue appeared?
Contradiction: what smallest fact would resolve the conflict?
Alternative: build a rival explanation using the same evidence.
Core theory: combine six lenses into a provisional story.
Base-rate check: compare with a boring common explanation.
Missing evidence: name the absent record or artifact that matters most.
Contamination risk: show how weak clues can look stronger.
Prediction: write one risky prediction the theory makes.
Disconfirmation: what evidence would damage the favorite explanation?
Chain of custody: where could meaning change from event to interpretation?
Confidence split: which link limits the whole case?
Motive caution: explain why motive alone is not proof.
Decision threshold: action, inquiry, or dismissal?
Peer challenge: keep the objection that survives your best reply.
Revision audit: change one chip and identify which conclusion changes.

apps.name: Heist Ledger
apps.summary: Plan a tiny heist by balancing crew roles, route pressure, tools, decoys, escape costs, and betrayal risks.
apps.description: A playful local strategy lab. Build a caper from six lenses, then pressure-test timing, suspicion, resource tradeoffs, getaway routes, failure recovery, and whether the prize was worth it.

config.instructions: Pick one chip from each heist lens to build the job. Add or swap complications to test route, heat, crew trust, escape, and the final score.
prompts: Final score: state the target, route, tool, heat level, trust break, escape choice, and whether the prize was worth the cost.
Briefing: define the prize, the crew role that matters most, and the one rule nobody should break.
Scout route: map entry, obstacle, blind spot, and exit. Which selected detail makes the route fragile?
Heat clock: turn the constraint into a countdown with a visible consequence for every delay.
Tool tradeoff: make the chosen tool solve one problem while creating another clue or suspicion.
Crew pressure: give two crew members incompatible priorities and decide who gets overruled.
First plan: combine six lenses into a three-step caper with owner, timing, decoy, and fallback.
Suspicion spike: assume a guard, rival, or witness notices the wrong thing. What harmless-looking action lowers heat?
Decoy test: use one selected item as a distraction. What must remain true for the decoy not to become evidence?
Betrayal risk: one crew member has a private reason to change the plan. How does the job reveal it without stopping?
Route collapse: close the easy exit and design a smaller, riskier path that preserves the prize or saves the crew.
Inventory crunch: remove one tool or disguise. Which objective gets abandoned, and which one becomes easier?
Split-second choice: choose between speed, silence, and loyalty. Assign a cost that shows up later.
Rival move: let the rival crew accidentally help and deliberately endanger the plan in the same beat.
Getaway math: compare the clean escape, loud escape, and no-prize escape. Which is actually a win?
Aftermath signal: name the clue investigators find first and the false story that buys time.
Crew debrief: identify the earliest bad assumption, the best improvisation, and the apology still owed.
Remix audit: swap one chip and rebuild the caper so every consequence still follows.
